content.claim: Removal of high-performing students from ordinary primary schools at ages 10 and 12 is asserted to create negative externalities for peer composition and teacher incentives in sending schools, but this 'brain drain' effect remains unquantified in available sources.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Education finance lens
limitations: Asserted by debate participant but not empirically measured in retrieved literature
No causal estimate of magnitude of peer effect or incentive impact on sending schools
International tracking literature on peer effects exists but Hungary-specific application unstudied
Requires primary data collection on school composition changes and teacher outcomes
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
